papers.title: Massive runaway stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud
papers.author_names: V.V. Gvaramadze P. Kroupa J. Pflamm-Altenburg

abstract.text: The origin of massive field stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) has long been an enigma. The recent measurements of large offsets (~100 km/s) between the heliocentric radial velocities of some very massive (O2-type) field stars and the systemic LMC velocity provides a possible explanation of this enigma and suggests that the field stars are runaway stars ejected from their birth places at the very beginning of their parent cluster's dynamical evolution. A straightforward way to prove this explanation is to measure the proper motions of the field stars and to show that they are moving away from one of the nearby star clusters or OB associations. This approach however is complicated by the large distance to the LMC, which makes accurate proper motion measurements difficult. We use an alternative approach for solving the problem, based on the search for bow shocks produced by runaway stars. The geometry of detected bow shocks would allow us to infer the direction of stellar motion and thereby to determine their possible parent clusters. In this paper we present the results of a search for bow shocks around six massive field stars which were suggested in the literature as candidate runaway stars. Using archival (Spitzer Space Telescope) data, we found a bow shock associated with one of our program stars, the O2 V((f*)) star BI 237, which is the first-ever detection of bow shocks in the LMC. Orientation of the bow shock suggests that BI 237 was ejected from the OB association LH 82 (located at ~120 pc in projection from the star). A by-product of our search is the detection of bow shocks generated by four OB stars in the field of the LMC and an arc-like structure attached to the candidate luminous blue variable R81 (HD 269128). The geometry of two of these bow shocks is consistent with the possibility that their associated stars were ejected from the 30 Doradus star forming complex.

brief.tldr: [observational astrophysics research paper] The paper presents the first detection of a bow shock in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), associated with the O2 V((f*)) star BI 237, providing direct evidence that it is a runaway star ejected from the OB association LH 82. The shock's orientation confirms its ejection direction, supporting the hypothesis that massive field stars originate in clusters and are dynamically ejected early in cluster evolution. Additional bow shocks are found near the 30 Doradus complex, suggesting similar dynamical ejection processes in that star-forming region.

source_fields.title: Massive runaway stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper presents the first detection of bow shocks around field OB stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud, providing direct evidence for the runaway nature of these stars and addressing the problem of their origin in the absence of precise proper motion data. Discussion and conclusion: The presence of a bow shock around BI 237 indicates it is a runaway O2-type star with a high peculiar velocity, supporting the conclusion that massive field stars in the LMC are likely ejected via primordial dynamical interactions rather than binary supernovae, and that mass segregation in young clusters begins early, not through Spitzer instability. Search for bow shocks in the LMC: A search for bow shocks around massive runaway stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud using Spitzer MIPS 24 μm data detected a bow shock associated with BI 237, while no shocks were found around the other stars, consistent with the low observed fraction of runaway OB stars that produce detectable bow shocks. Very massive field stars as runaways: The presence of very massive field stars in the LMC at large distances from clusters and with high peculiar velocities suggests they are likely runaway stars ejected from their birth clusters, supported by analogies to known Galactic runaway stars with bow shocks or velocity offsets.

title: Massive runaway stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents observational search for bow shocks around six candidate runaway stars using archival Spitzer data, with detection of bow shock for BI 237 and other OB stars
